Ligaments**: **Lateral (temporomandibular)** - limits posterior condyle movement. **Sphenomandibular, Stylomandibular** - accessory. **MOVEMENTS**: **Opening**: (1) **Rotation** (0-25mm) - condyle rotates in lower compartment (hinge), (2) **Translation** (>25mm) - condyle + disc glide forward along articular eminence (upper compartment). **Closing**: Reverse. **COMMON TMJ DISORDERS (TMD)**: **1. INTERNAL DERANGEMENT**: **Disc displacement**. **a) Disc displacement with reduction**: Disc **anteriorly displaced** (mouth closed), **clicks/pops** during opening (disc reduces back onto condyle), **full mouth opening**. **Reciprocal click** (opening + closing). **b) Disc displacement without reduction** (closed lock): Disc **permanently displaced anteriorly**, **cannot reduce**, **limited mouth opening** (<30mm), **deviation** to affected side, **no click** (disc stuck). **2. OSTEOARTHRITIS (Degenerative joint disease)**: **Crepitus** (grating sound), pain, limited movement, **radiographic changes** (flattening, osteophytes, sclerosis). **3. MYOFASCIAL PAIN DYSFUNCTION** (MPD): **Muscle pain** (masticatory muscles - masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), **tenderness** on palpation, **limited opening** (muscle spasm), **no joint sounds**. **Etiology**: Stress, bruxism, parafunctional habits. **4. ANKYLOSIS**: **Fusion** of joint - fibrous or bony. **No movement**, severely limited opening. **Causes**: Trauma, infection. **5. TMJ ARTHRITIS**: **Rheumatoid arthritis**, **Septic arthritis**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** (history, examination - opening, palpation, sounds), **Radiographs** (OPG, lateral TMJ), **MRI** (gold standard - soft tissue/disc visualization), **CT** (bone changes).

questions.question: What is the management of temporomandibular disorders (TMD)?
questions.answer: **TMD management** - **conservative first**, surgery **last resort**. **90% improve** with non-surgical therapy. **CONSERVATIVE (First-line)**: **1. Patient education**: Explain condition (usually self-limiting), avoid aggravating factors. **2. Behavior modification**: (a) **Soft diet** - avoid hard, chewy foods, (b) **Avoid wide opening** - no yawning, large bites, (c) **Avoid parafunctional habits** - **no gum chewing**, nail biting, pencil biting, (d) **Stress reduction** - counseling, relaxation techniques. **3. Physical therapy**: (a) **Moist heat** - warm compress (20 min, 3-4x/day) - muscle relaxation, (b) **Ice packs** - acute pain (first 24-48 hrs), (c) **Jaw exercises** - gentle stretching (improve mobility, muscle strength), (d) **Massage** - masticatory muscles (masseter, temporalis). **4. Pharmacotherapy**: (a) **NSAIDs** - **Ibuprofen 400mg TDS** (pain, inflammation), (b) **Muscle relaxants** - **Diazepam 5mg** or **Chlorzoxazone** (muscle spasm, especially nighttime bruxism), (c) **Tricyclic antidepressants** - **Amitriptyline 10-25mg** (chronic pain, nocturnal bruxism). **5. Occlusal splint (Bite guard)**: **Stabilization splint** - **hard acrylic**, covers maxillary/mandibular teeth, worn **nighttime**. **Functions**: (a) Protects teeth from bruxism, (b) **Repositions** condyle-disc relationship (controversial), (c) **Muscle relaxation** (reduces hyperactivity), (d) **Cognitive awareness** (reminds patient not to clench). **Indications**: Bruxism, muscle pain, mild disc displacement with reduction. **MINIMALLY INVASIVE**: **6. Arthrocentesis**: **Lavage** of upper joint space (wash out inflammatory mediators, lyse adhesions). **Indications**: **Closed lock** (disc displacement without reduction - acute), limited opening, pain. **Procedure**: **2 needles** in superior joint space, irrigate with **Ringer's lactate** (200-300 mL). **Success**: 70-80% (improves opening, reduces pain). **7. Arthroscopy**: **Endoscopic** visualization + treatment (lysis of adhesions, disc repositioning). More invasive than arthrocentesis. **SURGICAL (Last resort - <5% cases)**: **8. Discectomy**: Remove damaged disc. **9. Condylotomy**: Osteotomy of condylar neck (repositions condyle). **10. Joint replacement**: Total joint prosthesis (severe ankylosis, end-stage DJD). **STEPWISE APPROACH**: Conservative (2-3 months) → Arthrocentesis/Arthroscopy → Surgery (if all fail).

questions.question: What is TMJ dislocation? How do you reduce it?
questions.answer: **TMJ dislocation** - **condyle displaced** anteriorly **beyond articular eminence**, **cannot return** to glenoid fossa. **Bilateral** more common than unilateral. **ETIOLOGY**: **Wide opening** - yawning, laughing, prolonged dental treatment, trauma, seizures. **Predisposing**: **Lax ligaments**, **shallow fossa**, **previous dislocations**. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Mouth wide open** - **cannot close**. **2. Drooling** - cannot swallow saliva. **3. Speech difficulty**. **4. Bilateral**: **Prognathic** appearance (chin protrudes forward). **Preauricular depressions** (empty fossa palpable). **5. Unilateral**: **Deviation** to non-dislocated side, **open bite** opposite side. **6. Muscles in spasm** - painful, difficult to reduce (time-dependent). **7. Preauricular pain**. **TYPES**: **Acute** (<24-48 hrs), **Chronic/Recurrent** (repeated episodes), **Chronic** (>1 month unreduced). **REDUCTION (Manual - Hippocratic method)**: **1. Position**: Patient **seated** (head supported), operator **in front** or **behind** patient (behind easier). **2. Muscle relaxation**: (a) **Reassure** patient (anxiety worsens spasm), (b) **Local anesthesia** - inject into lateral pterygoid muscle/joint capsule (optional), (c) **Sedation/GA** if severe spasm (chronic dislocation), (d) **Wait** 10-15 min (muscle relaxation/fatigue). **3. Thumb placement**: Operator places **thumbs** on **occlusal surfaces** of lower molars (wrap thumbs with gauze - prevent bite injury). **Fingers** grasp **inferior border** of mandible externally. **4. Downward pressure**: **Press down firmly** on molars (disimpacts condyle from anterior to eminence, moves condyle **inferiorly**). **5. Backward rotation**: While maintaining downward pressure, **push chin backward and upward** (guides condyle **posteriorly** into fossa). **6. Reduction**: **Palpable/audible click** - condyle relocates. **Mouth closes** (patient bites down). **Quickly remove thumbs** (reflex bite). **POST-REDUCTION**: (1) **Soft diet** 1 week, (2) **Avoid wide opening** 2 weeks, (3) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (4) **Jaw exercises** - prevent stiffness. **PREVENTION OF RECURRENCE**: **1. Patient education** - avoid wide opening, yawning with care. **2. Occlusal splint** (night guard). **3. Sclerosing injections** - into capsule (controversial). **4. Surgical** (recurrent cases): **a) Eminectomy** - flatten/remove articular eminence (condyle cannot lock anteriorly), **b) Capsulorrhaphy** - tighten capsule. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Fracture** (forced reduction), **Nerve injury**, **Recurrence** (most common).

questions.question: Classify mandibular fractures. What is favorable vs unfavorable fracture?
questions.answer: **ANATOMICAL CLASSIFICATION**: **1. Symphysis**: Midline (between mental foramina). **2. Parasymphysis**: Between midline and canine. **3. Body**: Between canine and angle. **4. Angle**: Junction of body and ramus (most common - 25-30%). **Tooth in fracture line**, thin bone. **5. Ramus**: Ascending vertical portion. **6. Condyle**: Condylar neck/head (2nd most common - 25%). **Subcondylar, condylar neck, intracapsular**. **7. Coronoid process**: Rare (<1%). **DIRECTION OF FRACTURE LINE**: **FAVORABLE vs UNFAVORABLE** (muscle forces on fracture stability): **FAVORABLE**: Fracture line oriented such that **muscle pull** tends to **CLOSE/APPROXIMATE** fragments (stable). Easier to reduce/maintain. **UNFAVORABLE**: Muscle pull **DISTRACTS/SEPARATES** fragments (unstable). Requires rigid fixation. **HORIZONTAL FAVORABLE**: **Anterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **suprahyoid muscles** (digastric, geniohyoid). **Posterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **elevators** (masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid). **Fracture line** runs **anterior-inferior to posterior-superior** - fragments compress together. **Example**: Angle fracture with oblique line. **HORIZONTAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Fracture line** runs **anterior-superior to posterior-inferior** - muscle pull **separates** fragments (gap formation). **Example**: Body fracture with reverse oblique. **VERTICAL FAVORABLE**: **Medial pterygoid** pull (medial) on posterior fragment does NOT displace (fracture line orientation prevents). **VERTICAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Lateral pterygoid** + **medial pterygoid** pull causes **rotation/displacement** of posterior fragment **medially** and **anteriorly**. **OTHER CLASSIFICATIONS**: **1. Dentate vs Edentulous**: **Edentulous** - atrophic bone, higher fracture risk, different fixation. **2. Simple vs Compound**: **Compound** - communicates with external environment (oral cavity/skin) - higher infection risk, needs antibiotics. **Simple** - closed fracture. **3. Complete vs Incomplete** (greenstick). **4. Comminuted**: Multiple fragments. **5. Pathological**: Through diseased bone (cyst, tumor, osteomyelitis). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Unfavorable** fractures need **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation). **Favorable** may respond to **closed reduction** (IMF).

questions.question: Describe Le Fort classification of maxillary fractures.
questions.answer: **Le Fort classification** - **midface fractures** involving **maxilla**. Based on **predictable lines of weakness**. **KEY FEATURE**: **ALL Le Fort fractures** involve **pterygoid plates** (posterior maxilla) - **pathognomonic**. **LE FORT I** (Horizontal/Floating palate): **Level**: **Low** (above roots of teeth). **Fracture line**: Transverse across **maxilla above apices** of teeth, through **lateral nasal walls**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates** (posterior). **Result**: **Entire maxilla** (palate + alveolus) **separates** as single unit ("floating palate"). **Mobility**: **Maxilla** mobile (grasp anterior teeth, move up-down - free-floating). **Nasal bones** intact. **LE FORT II** (Pyramidal): **Level**: **Mid-level**. **Fracture line**: **Pyramid-shaped** - starts at **nasal bridge** (nasofrontal suture), extends **laterally** through **medial orbital walls** (lacrimal bones), **inferior orbital rim**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates**. **Result**: **Central maxilla** + **nose** detach as **pyramid-shaped segment**. **Mobility**: **Nose** + **maxilla** move together (grasp nose, move - entire central midface mobile). **Orbital rim** fractured. **LE FORT III** (Craniofacial dysjunction): **Level**: **High** (complete separation of facial skeleton from cranium). **Fracture line**: Across **nasofrontal suture**, through **medial** and **lateral orbital walls** (both), across **zygomatic arches**, through **pterygoid plates**, through **ethmoid sinuses**. **Result**: **ENTIRE FACE** separates from skull base (craniofacial separation). **Mobility**: **Entire midface** mobile (grasp face, moves as unit - "dish-face"). **Most severe**. **CLINICAL SIGNS (Common to all)**: **1. Malocclusion** - teeth don't meet properly (open bite, crossbite). **2. Facial swelling/edema** - extensive. **3. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding. **4. CSF rhinorrhea** (Le Fort II/III - cribriform plate involvement). **5. Ecchymosis** - periorbital ("raccoon eyes"), subconjunctival hemorrhage. **6. Mobility** - grasp maxilla, move (check level). **7. Step deformity** - palpable at fracture sites (infraorbital rim). **8. Anesthesia** - infraorbital nerve (V2) - cheek, upper lip, nose. **SPECIFIC SIGNS**: **Le Fort I**: Mobile maxilla, **nasal bones stable**. **Le Fort II**: **Bilateral periorbital edema**, **CSF leak** (may), **nasal bones mobile**. **Flattened nasal bridge**. **Le Fort III**: **Massive facial edema**, **"Dish-face"** deformity (flattened midface), **CSF leak** (high risk), **entire face mobile**. **Life-threatening** - airway compromise. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** + **CT scan** (gold standard - 3D reconstruction shows fracture lines). **MANAGEMENT**: **Stabilize airway** (priority - Le Fort III often needs intubation/tracheostomy), **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation with plates/screws), **IMF** (inter-maxillary fixation - 4-6 weeks).

questions.question: What are the principles of fracture management? Describe IMF and ORIF.
questions.answer: **FRACTURE MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES**: **1. Airway** - ensure patent airway (priority in trauma). **2. Hemorrhage control** - stop bleeding. **3. Soft tissue management** - clean, debride, suture lacerations. **4. Reduction** - restore anatomic position. **5. Fixation/Immobilization** - maintain reduction during healing. **6. Nutrition** - adequate (liquid diet if IMF). **7. Function** - restore occlusion, jaw movement. **8. Esthetics** - restore facial contour. **METHODS OF FIXATION**: **1. CLOSED REDUCTION** (Conservative): **No surgical exposure** of fracture. **Indirect manipulation** to reduce, then immobilize. **INTERMAXILLARY FIXATION (IMF / Maxillomandibular Fixation MMF)**: **Principle**: **Wire/elastics** between **maxillary and mandibular teeth** to **immobilize mandible** using maxilla as stable base. **Indications**: (a) **Favorable fractures** (stable), (b) **Minimally displaced** fractures, (c) **Condylar fractures** (conservative management), (d) **Adjunct to ORIF** (maintain occlusion during healing). **Methods**: **a) Arch bars + wires**: **Arch bars** (metal bars with hooks) **bonded/wired** to teeth (maxilla + mandible). **Intermaxillary wires/elastics** between hooks (fixes jaws together). **Standard** method. **b) Ivy loops**: **Individual wire loops** around teeth (instead of arch bars). **Quicker** but less stable. **c) Ernst ligatures, Stout wires**: Variations. **Duration**: **4-6 weeks** (mandible), **6-8 weeks** (maxilla - slower healing). **Complications**: **Aspiration risk** (vomiting - cannot open mouth), **Weight loss**, **Poor oral hygiene**, **TMJ stiffness**, **Airway compromise**. **Wire cutters** kept with patient always (emergency release). **2. OPEN REDUCTION INTERNAL FIXATION (ORIF)**: **Surgical exposure** of fracture, **anatomic reduction**, **rigid fixation** with **plates and screws** (internal). **Indications**: (a) **Unfavorable** fractures (unstable), (b) **Displaced** fractures, (c) **Multiple** fractures, (d) **Comminuted** fractures, (e) **Edentulous** mandible (no teeth for IMF), (f) **Bilateral** fractures (both sides), (g) **Condylar fractures** with malocclusion/dislocation. **Advantages**: (1) **Rigid fixation** - immediate stability, (2) **Early function** - no/minimal IMF needed, (3) **Better alignment** - direct visualization, (4) **Shorter treatment** - faster return to normal. **Technique**: (a) **Approach** - intraoral or extraoral incision, (b) **Expose fracture** - elevate soft tissue, (c) **Reduce** - align fragments anatomically, (d) **Fix** - **Titanium miniplates + screws** across fracture line. **Champy's principles**: **Load-bearing** (body, angle) - 2 plates. **Tension zone** (superior border), **Compression zone** (inferior border). (e) **Closure** - layer by layer. **Complications**: **Infection** (plate exposure), **Malunion**, **Nerve injury**, **Hardware failure**, **Plate removal** (later if symptomatic). **3. EXTERNAL FIXATION**: **Pins** through skin into bone, connected externally. **Rare** in facial fractures (cosmetic concerns). Used in **comminuted**, **infected**, **gunshot wounds**. **CHOICE**: **Favorable/Simple** → **Closed + IMF**. **Unfavorable/Complex** → **ORIF** (± short IMF).

questions.question: What is a zygomatic complex (tripod) fracture? How is it diagnosed and managed?
questions.answer: **Zygomatic complex (ZMC) / Tripod fracture** - fracture of **zygoma** (cheekbone) with **separation** at **3 sutures**: (1) **Zygomaticofrontal** (lateral orbital rim), (2) **Zygomaticomaxillary** (inferior orbital rim), (3) **Zygomaticotemporal** (zygomatic arch). **Etiology**: **Direct trauma** to cheek - assault (fist/blunt object), RTA, sports. **Zygoma prominence** makes it vulnerable. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Flattening of cheek** - **loss of malar prominence** (depressed zygoma). **2. Periorbital edema/ecchymosis** - swelling, black eye. **3. Subconjunctival hemorrhage** - blood in sclera (no posterior limit - differentiates from localized bruise). **4. Step deformity** - palpable at **infraorbital rim** and **lateral orbital rim** (fracture displacement). **5. Infraorbital nerve anesthesia** - **numbness** of cheek, upper lip, nose, upper teeth (nerve injury in infraorbital canal). **Pathognomonic sign**. **6. Enophthalmos** - **sunken eye** (if orbital floor fractured - orbital volume increased). **Late sign** (after edema subsides). **7. Diplopia** - **double vision** (extraocular muscle entrapment in orbital floor fracture, or edema). **8. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (if arch impinges on coronoid process). **9. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding (maxillary sinus involvement). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic features. **2. Radiographs**: **Waters view** (occipitomental) - best for zygoma. **Shows**: (a) **Opacification** of maxillary sinus (blood), (b) **Fracture lines** at sutures, (c) **Depressed arch**. **3. CT scan** - **gold standard** (3D reconstruction - exact displacement, orbital floor involvement). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Persistent enophthalmos**, **Persistent diplopia** (muscle entrapment), **Facial asymmetry** (malunion), **Infraorbital anesthesia** (may be permanent). **MANAGEMENT**: **OBSERVATION** (No treatment): **Indications**: (a) **Minimal/no displacement**, (b) **No functional impairment** (vision, occlusion), (c) **No esthetic concern**. **Monitor** - most heal uneventfully. **SURGICAL REDUCTION**: **Indications**: (a) **Significant displacement** (cosmetic deformity), (b) **Enophthalmos** (>2mm), (c) **Persistent diplopia** (>2 weeks), (d) **Trismus**, (e) **Large orbital floor defect**. **Timing**: **Early** (within 7-14 days - before healing/fibrosis). **Techniques**: (a) **Closed reduction**: **Gillies approach** - temporal incision, **elevator** under zygomatic arch, **lift** zygoma (pop back into place). **No fixation**. Simple fractures only. (b) **Open reduction**: **Incisions** (intraoral + lower eyelid OR coronal), **expose fracture** sites, **reduce** anatomically, **fix** with **miniplates** at 2-3 points (zygomaticomaxillary buttress, infraorbital rim, zygomaticofrontal). **Orbital floor reconstruction** if large defect (implant - titanium mesh/porous polyethylene). **Post-op**: **Antibiotics** (sinus communication), **Decongestants** (nasal), **Avoid nose blowing**, **Eye care** (if eyelid swelling).

questions.question: What is the management of condylar fractures?
questions.answer: **Condylar fractures** - **2nd most common** mandibular fracture (25%). **Bilateral** common (falls on chin - force transmitted to both condyles). **TYPES**: **1. Intracapsular** - fracture within TMJ capsule (condylar head). **2. Condylar neck** - below head, above sigmoid notch. **3. Subcondylar** - below sigmoid notch (most common). **DISPLACEMENT**: **Non-displaced/minimally displaced** vs **Displaced** vs **Dislocated** (complete loss of contact). **GENERAL PRINCIPLE**: **MOST condylar fractures** managed **CONSERVATIVELY** (non-surgical). **INDICATIONS FOR CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT** (90%): **1. Non-displaced/minimally displaced** fractures. **2. Bilateral fractures** with **acceptable occlusion**. **3. Condylar neck fractures** (below head). **4. Children** (excellent remodeling potential - almost always conservative). **5. Edentulous patients**. **6. Unilateral** with normal occlusion. **CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT**: **1. Short-term IMF** (if needed): **7-14 days** only (prevent ankylosis - TMJ stiffness). **Training elastics** (light - guide occlusion, allow some movement). OR **No IMF** if occlusion acceptable. **2. Soft diet**: 4-6 weeks. **3. Early mobilization**: **Jaw exercises** start after **1-2 weeks** (crucial - prevent ankylosis, restore function). **Active** opening/closing, lateral movements. **4. Analgesics**: NSAIDs. **5. Follow-up**: Monitor occlusion, mouth opening, healing (radiographs). **OUTCOMES**: **Excellent** remodeling, functional recovery. **INDICATIONS FOR SURGICAL (ORIF)** (10%): **ABSOLUTE**: **1. Bilateral fractures** with **malocclusion** (anterior open bite - condyles telescoped). **2. Condyle displaced into middle cranial fossa** (intracranial). **3. Foreign body** in joint. **4. Lateral extracapsular dislocation** (condyle outside fossa laterally). **RELATIVE**: **1. Severe displacement** (>45° angulation, >1cm overlap). **2. Inability to achieve occlusion** conservatively. **3. Associated midface fractures** (need stable mandible for MMF). **SURGICAL APPROACHES**: **Preauricular**, **Submandibular**, **Retromandibular**, **Endoscopic**. **Fix** with miniplate. **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Ankylosis** - especially if prolonged IMF (>2-3 weeks). **Prevention**: Early mobilization. **2. Malocclusion** - managed with occlusal adjustment, orthodontics. **3. Facial nerve injury** (surgical cases - temporary usually). **4. TMJ dysfunction** - pain, clicking (10-20% - usually resolves). **5. Growth disturbance** (children - rare with modern conservative management). **KEY**: **Children** - almost **NEVER** operate (excellent remodeling). **Adults** - **conservative** unless absolute indication.

questions.question: What is sialolithiasis? Describe clinical features and management.
questions.answer: **Sialolithiasis** - **salivary calculus (stone)** in ductal system of salivary gland. **Most common salivary gland disorder**. **LOCATION**: **Submandibular gland/duct** (80-90%) - **Wharton's duct**. **Reasons**: (1) **Upward flow** (against gravity), (2) **Thick alkaline** secretion, (3) **Higher calcium/phosphate** content, (4) **Long tortuous** duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swelling** of gland **during meals** (saliva production), **subsides** after meal (1-2 hrs). **"Mealtime syndrome"**. **2. Palpable stone** - **bimanual palpation** (submandibular duct - floor of mouth). **Hard, immobile** mass along duct. **3. Reduced salivary flow** - thick/absent saliva from duct orifice (milk duct). **4. Tenderness** - over gland/duct. **5. Secondary infection** - if complete obstruction → **acute sialadenitis** (fever, swelling, pus from duct). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - mealtime swelling + palpable stone. **2. Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium). **Sialography** (contrast in duct - shows stone as **filling defect**) - **AVOID if acute infection** (spread infection). **3. Ultrasound** - detects radiolucent stones, ductal dilation. **4. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic visualization + removal. **MANAGEMENT**: **MEDICAL** (Small stones <3mm, distal duct): **1. Conservative**: (a) **Hydration** - increase saliva flow, (b) **Sialogogues** - lemon juice, sour candies (stimulate saliva), (c) **Warm compresses** + **Massage** - milk stone distally, (d) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (e) **Antibiotics** (if infection) - **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** (covers *S. aureus, Streptococcus*). **2. Stone milking** - **bimanual massage** (push stone anteriorly toward orifice, express). **SURGICAL**: **1. Intraoral removal** (Distal duct stones - palpable in floor of mouth): (a) **Anesthesia** (local), (b) **Dilate duct** orifice, (c) **Incision** over stone (longitudinal - along duct), (d) **Remove stone**, (e) **Marsupialize** duct (suture duct edges to mucosa - create wide opening, prevent stricture). **Do NOT suture duct** closed (stenosis risk). **2. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic **basket retrieval** or **lithotripsy** (break stone). **Minimally invasive**. **3. Gland excision** - **Submandibular gland removal** if: (a) **Recurrent** sialolithiasis, (b) **Intraglandular** stone (within gland), (c) **Chronic sialadenitis** (gland damaged). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury**, **Hypoglossal nerve injury**, **Marginal mandibular nerve** (facial nerve branch - lip droop). **PREVENTION**: Hydration, good oral hygiene.

questions.question: What is a ranula? Describe types and management.
questions.answer: **Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth). **Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog). **TYPES**: **1. SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle). **Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces tongue** upward/laterally (if large), (d) **No pain** (unless infected). **2. PLUNGING (Cervical) RANULA**: **Extends** through mylohyoid muscle into **neck** (submandibular/submental space). **"Plunges"** below mylohyoid. **Clinical features**: (a) **Neck swelling** (submandibular region), (b) **Soft, fluctuant**, non-tender, (c) **May** have oral component (floor of mouth swelling) OR **only neck** swelling, (d) **Differential**: Dermoid cyst, lymphangioma, cystic hygroma, branchial cyst. **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic bluish oral swelling (floor of mouth). **2. Aspiration** - **thick, ropy, mucoid** fluid (saliva - viscous, egg-white consistency). **Differential from epidermoid** (cheesy material). **3. MRI/CT** - plunging ranula (extent, relation to mylohyoid). **4. NO histopathology** - **pseudocyst** (no epithelial lining - just mucus pool in connective tissue). **MANAGEMENT**: **SIMPLE RANULA**: **1. Observation** - may spontaneously resolve (especially small, recent). **2. Marsupialization**: (a) **Elliptical excision** of cyst roof (create wide opening into mouth), (b) **Suture** cyst lining edges to oral mucosa, (c) **Simple**, outpatient procedure. **Limitation**: **High recurrence** (50%) - gland continues secreting. **3. Excision of sublingual gland**: **Definitive treatment** - **remove entire sublingual gland** (source of saliva). (a) **Intraoral approach**, (b) **Excise sublingual gland** completely (along with ranula), (c) **Low recurrence** (5-10%). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury** (runs close - numbness of tongue), **Wharton's duct injury** (submandibular duct - runs through floor of mouth). **Preferred** for recurrent cases. **PLUNGING RANULA**: **Sublingual gland excision** via **intraoral** approach (addresses source). **Neck dissection** usually **not needed** (ranula collapses once gland removed). **Alternative**: **Combined intraoral + neck approach** if large cervical component. **RECURRENCE**: **Marsupialization** 50%, **Gland excision** 5-10%.

questions.question: How do you manage dental extractions in patients with bleeding disorders?
questions.answer: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1. History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets). **2. Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels. **Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin). **General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**. **3. Physician consultation**: Coordinate with hematologist/physician. **HEMOPHILIA**: **Factor replacement** essential. **1. Mild Hemophilia A** (Factor VIII 5-25%): **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** - releases stored Factor VIII (2-3x increase). **Dose**: 0.3 mcg/kg IV **1 hr before** procedure. **Alternative**: **Tranexamic acid** (antifibrinolytic) alone. **2. Moderate/Severe Hemophilia** (Factor VIII <5%): **Factor VIII concentrate** - raise level to **50-100%** before extraction (target depends on extent). **Dose**: **Loading**: 25-50 IU/kg pre-op, **Maintenance**: Continue post-op (7-10 days). **Hemophilia B**: **Factor IX concentrate** (similar protocol). **3. Local measures** (crucial): (a) **Atraumatic extraction** - minimize trauma, (b) **Sutures** - **primary closure** (figure-of-eight over socket), (c) **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel)**, **Gelfoam**, **Fibrin glue** - pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **5% TXA** rinse QID x 7 days (prevents clot lysis), (e) **Avoid** aspirin, NSAIDs (use paracetamol). **4. Hospital setting** - NOT outpatient (need monitoring, factor availability). **WARFARIN (Anticoagulation)**: **1. Check INR**: **Therapeutic range** (2-3) - **safe** for simple extractions with local measures. **INR 2.5-3.5** - acceptable for minor oral surgery. **INR >4** - **defer**, adjust warfarin (consult physician). **2. DO NOT STOP warfarin** (risk of thromboembolism - stroke, MI). **Exception**: Major surgery (consult physician - may bridge with heparin). **3. Local hemostatic measures**: (a) **Minimize trauma** - atraumatic technique, **limit extractions** (1-2 teeth per visit), (b) **Sutures** - resorbable, primary closure, (c) **Hemostatic agents** - **Surgicel**, **Gelfoam** pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **10 mL 5% TXA** rinse **QID x 5-7 days** (do NOT swallow - hold 2 min, spit). **VERY effective**. (e) **Pressure** - bite gauze 30 min. **4. Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin** (reduce infection - infected socket bleeds more). **5. Post-op instructions**: **No rinsing** 24 hrs, **soft diet**, **avoid hot liquids**, **no alcohol** (interacts with warfarin). **6. Follow-up**: Day 1, day 3 (check bleeding). **ASPIRIN/CLOPIDOGREL**: **Continue medication** (DO NOT STOP - thrombotic risk). **Local measures** sufficient for simple extractions. **Clopidogrel** slightly higher bleeding risk than aspirin. **THROMBOCYTOPENIA**: **Platelet count <50,000** - **defer** elective extraction. **<50,000** - **platelet transfusion** pre-op (raise to >50,000). **LOCAL MEASURES** (universal - all bleeding disorders): Atraumatic technique, sutures, hemostatic packing, **tranexamic acid mouthwash** (CRITICAL), pressure, post-op care.

questions.question: Describe the phases of wound healing.
questions.answer: **Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity. **TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring. **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring. **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later. **PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1. HEMOSTASIS (Immediate - minutes)**: **Vasoconstriction** → **Platelet aggregation** → **Clot formation** (fibrin mesh). **Prevents blood loss**, **scaffolding** for cell migration. **2. INFLAMMATORY PHASE (0-3 days)**: **a) Early (0-24 hrs)**: **Vasodilation** → **Increased permeability** → **Edema**, **erythema**, **pain**. **Neutrophils** arrive (phagocytose bacteria, debris) - peak 24 hrs. **b) Late (24-72 hrs)**: **Macrophages** arrive (phagocytose debris, **secrete growth factors** - crucial for healing progression). **Lymphocytes** (immune response). **Clinical**: **Swelling, redness, warmth, pain, loss of function** (cardinal signs of inflammation). **Prolonged inflammation** → **chronic wound**, **delayed healing**. **3. PROLIFERATIVE PHASE (3 days - 3 weeks)**: **Tissue formation**. **a) Granulation tissue formation** (3-5 days): **Fibroblasts** proliferate → **collagen synthesis** (Type III initially). **Angiogenesis** → **new capillaries** (pink, granular appearance). **Myofibroblasts** → **wound contraction**. **b) Epithelialization** (24-48 hrs onwards): **Keratinocytes** migrate across wound (from edges) → **cover defect**. **Faster** in **primary intention** (sutured - edges close). **c) Contraction**: **Myofibroblasts** pull wound edges together (reduces defect size - important in **secondary intention**). **4. REMODELING/MATURATION PHASE (3 weeks - 1 year)**: **Collagen remodeling**: **Type III collagen** replaced by **Type I** (stronger). **Collagen** reorganized along **tension lines**. **Scar maturation**: Initially **red, raised** → gradually **pale, flat** (12-18 months). **Tensile strength** increases (plateaus at **80%** of original - never 100%). **Excess collagen** breakdown → **scar softens**. **FACTORS AFFECTING HEALING**: **Local**: Infection, blood supply, foreign body, wound tension, hematoma. **Systemic**: **Diabetes** (impaired), **Immunosuppression** (steroids, chemotherapy), **Malnutrition** (protein, Vitamin C deficiency), **Age** (elderly slower), **Smoking** (vasoconstriction). **NORMAL TIMELINE**: **Epithelialization** complete: **7-10 days** (oral mucosa faster - 3-5 days). **Suture removal**: **7 days** (face/oral), **10-14 days** (skin elsewhere). **Scar maturation**: **12-18 months**.

questions.question: Describe principles of suturing and commonly used suture materials.
questions.answer: **PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1. Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves. **2. Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats). **3. Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery). **4. Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma). **5. Eversion**: Wound edges **slightly everted** (not inverted) - better healing, less scar depression. **6. Tension-free**: **No tension** on wound edges (undermining if needed). **7. Accurate approximation**: **Align tissue layers** properly. **8. Knot security**: **Square knots** (surgeon's knot if tension). **SUTURE MATERIALS**: **ABSORBABLE** (degraded by body - no removal needed): **1. Catgut** (Plain/Chromic): **Natural** (collagen from sheep/cattle intestine). **Rapid absorption** (plain 7-10 days, chromic 14-21 days). **High tissue reaction**. **Rarely used** now. **2. Polyglycolic acid (Vicryl)**: **Synthetic**. **Absorption**: 60-90 days. **Moderate tissue reaction**. **Uses**: **Deep layers**, muscle, subcutaneous (where removal difficult). **3. Polyglactin (Vicryl Rapide)**: **Faster absorption** (10-14 days). **Uses**: **Oral mucosa** (intraoral - no removal needed). **NON-ABSORBABLE** (must be removed): **1. Silk**: **Natural** (braided). **Easy handling**, good knot security. **High tissue reaction** (inflammation). **Uses**: **Intraoral/skin** (extraoral) - **MOST COMMON** in oral surgery. **Removal**: **7 days**. **2. Nylon (Ethilon)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Low tissue reaction**. **Less memory** (harder to tie). **Uses**: Skin closure (face). **3. Polypropylene (Prolene)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Very low reaction**. **High memory** (springs back). **Uses**: Skin, cardiovascular. **4. Stainless steel**: **Very strong**, **inert**. **Difficult handling**. **Uses**: Bone wiring, tendon repair. **NEEDLE TYPES**: **Cutting** (triangular - cuts through tissue) - **SKIN, oral mucosa** (tough tissue). **Reverse cutting** (preferred - strength at wound edge). **Round (Tapered)** - muscle, viscera (soft tissue). **SIZE**: **Oral surgery**: **3-0** or **4-0** silk (moderate thickness). **Finer** (5-0, 6-0) for delicate (facial skin). **Thicker** (2-0, 0) for heavy tissue. **SUTURING TECHNIQUES**: **1. Simple interrupted**: **Individual knots** - each suture independent. **Most common**, versatile. **Advantage**: One fails, others hold. **2. Continuous (Running)**: **Single thread**, multiple passes, **one knot** at each end. **Faster**. **Disadvantage**: One break, entire suture fails. **Uses**: Long incisions. **3. Horizontal mattress**: **Everting** suture (good for edges). **Distributes tension**. **4. Vertical mattress**: **Deep + superficial** bites (two-layer closure in one). **Eversion**. **5. Figure-of-eight**: **Hemostatic** (compresses tissue). **Uses**: Extraction sockets, bleeding. **REMOVAL**: **Oral/face**: **7 days**. **Elsewhere**: **10-14 days**. **Absorbable**: **No removal** (falls off/dissolves).

questions.question: Classify nerve injuries. How do you assess and manage paresthesia?
questions.answer: **NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1. NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**. **Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged). **No Wallerian degeneration**. **Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks). **Prognosis**: Excellent. **2. AXONOTMESIS**: **Moderate**. **Axon disrupted**, **endoneurium intact** (nerve sheath preserved). **Wallerian degeneration** occurs distal to injury. **Regeneration**: **Yes** (axons regrow along intact sheath - **1mm/day** / **1 inch/month**). **Recovery**: **Partial to complete** (weeks-months). **Prognosis**: Good (80-90% recovery). **3. NEUROTMESIS**: **Severe**. **Complete transection** (axon + endoneurium + perineurium). **Wallerian degeneration**. **Regeneration**: **Poor** (axons cannot find pathway - **neuroma** formation, misdirection). **Recovery**: **Minimal** without surgical repair. **Prognosis**: Poor unless **microsurgical repair**. **DENTAL SURGERY NERVE INJURIES**: **Inferior alveolar nerve (IAN)**: 3rd molar extraction, implants, mandibular fractures. **Lingual nerve**: 3rd molar extraction (lingual flap elevation), lingual anesthesia. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.5-5%, **Permanent** 0.1-1%. **ASSESSMENT OF PARESTHESIA**: **1. History**: **Onset** (immediate post-op vs delayed), **Duration**, **Progression** (improving/static/worsening), **Nature** (numbness, tingling, dysesthesia - abnormal sensation). **2. Clinical tests**: **a) Two-point discrimination**: **Normal**: 2-4mm (lower lip/tongue). **Impaired**: >6mm. **b) Light touch**: Cotton wisp/brush - **detect sensation**. **c) Pin-prick**: **Sharp/dull** discrimination. **d) Thermal**: Hot/cold discrimination. **3. Map extent**: Mark borders of anesthetic/paresthetic area (compare to normal side). **4. Baseline documentation**: **Immediate post-op** assessment (medico-legal). **5. Serial assessment**: **1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months** (track recovery). **MANAGEMENT**: **IMMEDIATE (If injury suspected intra-op)**: **1. Inform patient** (document consent discussion). **2. Primary repair** (if complete transection visualized - rare) - **microsurgical** epineurial sutures. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **1. Reassurance**: **70-90%** spontaneous recovery (most are neuropraxia/axonotmesis). **Majority recover** in **3-6 months**. **2. Observation**: **First 3 months** - natural recovery expected. **Serial assessments**. **3. Pharmacotherapy** (controversial efficacy): **Vitamin B12** (Methylcobalamin 1500 mcg) - supports nerve regeneration. **Gabapentin/Pregabalin** - if neuropathic pain. **Corticosteroids** (Dexamethasone) - early (within 48 hrs) if severe injury (reduce edema) - **controversial**. **4. Microsurgical repair**: **Indications**: (a) **Complete transection** (known intra-op), (b) **No recovery** at **3-6 months** (likely neurotmesis), (c) **Worsening** paresthesia. **Timing**: **3-6 months** optimal (too early - may recover spontaneously, too late - distal nerve atrophy). **Technique**: **Direct repair** (if gap <5mm) or **Nerve graft** (if larger gap). **Prognosis**: Variable (30-70% improvement). **PREVENTION**: **Pre-operative CBCT** (assess IAN proximity in 3rd molar cases), **Careful technique**, **Lingual plate preservation**, **Avoid excessive force**.

questions.question: What are the indications for tracheostomy? Describe the procedure.
questions.answer: **Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access. **Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial burns, (e) **Angioedema** - severe allergic reaction. **2. Prolonged intubation**: **>7-10 days** mechanical ventilation (ICU patients) - **prevents laryngeal damage** from endotracheal tube. **3. Inability to intubate**: **Difficult airway** (limited mouth opening, anatomical anomalies). **4. Pulmonary toilet**: Facilitate **suctioning** of secretions (neuromuscular disorders, coma). **5. Prophylactic**: Before **major head-neck surgery** (airway edema expected post-op). **TYPES**: **Emergency** (cricothyrotomy - faster) vs **Elective** (tracheostomy - preferred if time permits). **PROCEDURE (Elective tracheostomy)**: **1. Position**: **Supine**, **neck extended** (shoulder roll), **head midline**. **2. Anesthesia**: **General** (preferred) or **Local** with sedation (awake tracheostomy). **3. Landmarks**: **Thyroid cartilage** (laryngeal prominence), **Cricoid cartilage** (below thyroid), **Tracheal rings** (below cricoid), **Sternal notch**. **4. Incision**: **Transverse** (2-3 cm) at level of **2nd-3rd tracheal ring** (midway between cricoid and sternal notch). **Alternative**: **Vertical** midline incision (faster - emergency). **5. Dissection**: (a) Divide **subcutaneous tissue**, **platysma** (muscle), (b) **Retract** strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid) **laterally**, (c) **Divide thyroid isthmus** (suture-ligate or retract superiorly) - exposes trachea. **6. Tracheal incision**: (a) **Identify tracheal rings** (palpate), (b) **Vertical incision** through **2nd-3rd rings** (inferiorly based flap OR cruciate incision OR window excision), (c) **Avoid 1st ring** (subglottic stenosis risk). **7. Tube insertion**: (a) **Dilate** tracheal opening (tracheal dilator), (b) Insert **tracheostomy tube** (cuffed, appropriate size - adult 7-9mm), (c) **Inflate cuff** (seal airway), (d) **Confirm**: Bilateral air entry (auscultation), **Capnography** (CO₂ detection). **8. Secure tube**: **Sutures** to skin (stay sutures through trachea for emergency re-insertion if displaced), **Ties** around neck (snug - 2 fingers space). **9. Dressing**: Sterile gauze around stoma. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Intra-operative**: Hemorrhage (thyroid vessels), **apnea** (hypercapnic patient - loss of respiratory drive), tube misplacement. **Early post-op**: **Tube obstruction** (secretions, blood), **accidental decannulation** (first 3-5 days dangerous - tract not formed, difficult re-insertion), **subcutaneous emphysema**, pneumothorax. **Late**: **Tracheal stenosis** (1st ring injury, high cuff pressure), **tracheoesophageal fistula**, granulation tissue. **POST-OP CARE**: **Humidified oxygen** (prevent secretion drying), **Regular suctioning**, **Tube change** (first change 5-7 days after tract established, then weekly), **Stoma care** (cleaning).

questions.question: Describe the extracranial course and branches of the facial nerve.
questions.answer: **Facial nerve (CN VII)** - **motor nerve** to muscles of **facial expression** + parasympathetic to salivary/lacrimal glands + taste (anterior 2/3 tongue via chorda tympani). **EXTRACRANIAL COURSE**: **1. Exit from skull**: Emerges from **stylomastoid foramen** (between styloid and mastoid processes). **2. Enters parotid gland**: Immediately enters **parotid gland** (runs through gland - **NOT supplies parotid** - supplied by glossopharyngeal CN IX). **3. Division**: Divides into **two main trunks** within parotid: **Temporofacial** (upper) and **Cervicofacial** (lower). **4. Further branching**: Forms **5 main terminal branches** (pes anserinus - "goose foot" pattern). **TERMINAL BRANCHES (memory: "**T**o **Z**anzibar **B**y **M**otor **C**ar")**: **1. TEMPORAL (Frontal)**: **Course**: Crosses **zygomatic arch** (superficial - vulnerable). **Supplies**: **Frontalis** (raises eyebrows), **orbicularis oculi** (upper part - eye closure), corrugator. **Injury**: **Inability to raise eyebrow**, wrinkle forehead. **At risk**: Trauma to temporal region, **parotidectomy**, **facelift surgery**. **2. ZYGOMATIC**: **Course**: Crosses **zygoma**. **Supplies**: **Orbicularis oculi** (lower part - eye closure). **Injury**: **Incomplete eye closure**, lagophthalmos (corneal exposure risk). **3. BUCCAL**: **Course**: Crosses **masseter muscle** (over buccal region). **Multiple branches** (interconnecting). **Supplies**: **Buccinator**, orbicularis oris (upper lip), muscles of nose. **Injury**: **Loss of smile** (upper lip), inability to puff cheeks (blow air). **At risk**: **Parotidectomy**, facial trauma. **LEAST serious** branch injury (multiple interconnections - compensation). **4. MARGINAL MANDIBULAR**: **Course**: Runs along **inferior border of mandible** (may dip **below** mandible up to 1 cm in 20% cases - danger zone). Crosses **facial vessels**. **Supplies**: **Lower lip** depressors (depressor labii inferioris, depressor anguli oris), mentalis. **Injury**: **Most CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT** - **asymmetric smile** (lower lip doesn't move down on affected side - "**lip droop**"), inability to show lower teeth, deviation of mouth to normal side. **At risk**: **Submandibular gland surgery**, **neck dissections**, **mandibular angle/body fracture surgery**, facelift. **5. CERVICAL**: **Course**: Runs **inferiorly** into neck. **Supplies**: **Platysma** (neck muscle). **Injury**: **Minimal clinical significance** (platysma not essential). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Parotid gland surgery** - nerve runs through gland (risk of injury to all branches). **Mandibular fractures/surgery** - marginal mandibular branch vulnerable. **Temporal artery biopsy** - temporal branch at risk. **PROTECTION during surgery**: **Identify nerve**, gentle tissue handling, **avoid cautery** near nerve.

questions.question: What are the essential elements of informed consent for oral surgery?
questions.answer: **Informed consent** - **legal and ethical** requirement before any surgical procedure. Patient must **understand** and **voluntarily agree** to treatment after receiving adequate information. **ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS**: **1. COMPETENCE**: Patient must be **legally competent** (adult >18 years, sound mind). **Exceptions**: **Minors** (<18 years) - **parental consent** required. **Emergency** - implied consent (save life). **Incompetent patient** - guardian/next-of-kin consent. **2. DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION**: **a) Diagnosis**: Patient's current condition (e.g., impacted tooth, abscess). **b) Proposed treatment**: Procedure planned (e.g., surgical extraction, I&D), **Alternative treatments** (e.g., observation, antibiotics), **Option of no treatment** (consequences). **c) Risks and complications**: **Common**: Pain, swelling, bleeding, infection, dry socket, trismus. **Serious** (even if rare): **Nerve injury** (IAN/lingual - numbness), **jaw fracture**, **sinus perforation**, **damage to adjacent teeth**, **TMJ problems**. **d) Benefits**: Expected outcome (pain relief, infection control, prevent complications). **e) Prognosis**: Expected recovery time, success rate. **3. UNDERSTANDING**: Patient must **comprehend** information (language appropriate, layman's terms - avoid medical jargon). **Verify understanding**: Ask patient to **repeat** key points. **Written + verbal** explanation (consent form + discussion). **4. VOLUNTARINESS**: Decision made **freely** without coercion, pressure, or undue influence. **5. AUTHORIZATION**: Patient **signs consent form** (written documentation). **DOCUMENTATION**: **1. Consent form**: **Signed**, **dated**, **witnessed**. **Contains**: Procedure name, risks disclosed, patient signature, doctor signature, witness. **2. Medical records**: **Note in records** - "Informed consent obtained, risks explained (nerve injury, bleeding, infection, dry socket, jaw fracture, sinus perforation), patient understood, all questions answered". **Specific to procedure** (e.g., for 3rd molar - mention IAN injury risk with percentage). **SPECIAL SITUATIONS**: **Emergency**: **Implied consent** (unconscious patient, life-threatening - no time for consent). **Document**: "Emergency treatment, unable to obtain consent, acted in patient's best interest". **Minors**: **Parental consent**. **Exception**: **Mature minor** (court-determined competence) or **emancipated minor** (married, self-supporting). **Language barrier**: **Interpreter** (document interpreter used). **WITHDRAWAL OF CONSENT**: Patient can **withdraw consent** at any time (even during procedure if conscious). Must **stop procedure** (unless emergency). **MEDICO-LEGAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Failure** to obtain informed consent = **BATTERY** (illegal touching) - grounds for lawsuit even if treatment successful. **Inadequate disclosure** = **NEGLIGENCE**. **Protection**: **Proper consent** + **documentation** protects against litigation. **WHAT TO DOCUMENT**: "**Discussed**: Procedure, alternatives, risks (bleeding, infection, nerve injury, dry socket), benefits, prognosis. **Patient verbalized understanding**, **all questions answered**, **consent form signed**".
